text: A training intensity distribution of ~80% below ventilatory threshold, ~12% between thresholds, and ~8% above respiratory compensation threshold produces greater performance improvements than ~67/25/8% distribution in subelite runners over 5 months.

Subelite runners following a polarized intensity distribution (80/12/8) improved 10.4 km cross-country performance by 157 seconds vs 121.5 seconds for a threshold-emphasis distribution (67/25/8), a statistically significant difference (p=0.03).

Increasing the proportion of moderate-intensity training (between ventilatory and respiratory compensation thresholds) at the expense of low-intensity training reduces performance gains even when high-intensity volume is held constant.

A sufficient contribution of high-intensity training (~8% of total volume above respiratory compensation threshold) is needed alongside high volumes of low-intensity work for optimal endurance performance gains.
Training intensity can be effectively controlled and quantified using heart rate zones anchored to ventilatory threshold and respiratory compensation threshold determined by maximal exercise testing.

Both polarized (~80% low intensity) and threshold-emphasis (~67% low intensity) training distributions produce meaningful performance improvements over 5 months in subelite runners, but the polarized approach yields ~30% greater improvement.
